data.title: Descript: Edit Video by Editing Text
data.excerpt: Descript is the AI video and podcast editor that lets you cut, trim, and polish by editing a transcript. Free to start — see why creators love it.

$media.alt: Descript: Edit Video by Editing Text

children.text: Disclosure: This post contains affiliate links. If you sign up through them, TonyCiencia may earn a commission at no extra cost to you.

I've been covering creative tools for years, and every once in a while one comes along that genuinely shifts how I think about a workflow. Descript is that tool for video and audio editing. If you've ever wished you could just delete a sentence from a recording the way you'd delete a sentence from a Word document, Descript makes that wish real.
Let me break down exactly what it does, who it's for, and why I keep recommending it.
What Is Descript?
Descript is an
AI-powered video and podcast editor

built around a simple but powerful idea: your recording's transcript *is* your editing timeline. Instead of scrubbing through waveforms and video frames to find the moment you want to cut, you read the transcript, highlight the words you want gone, and hit delete. The corresponding audio and video disappear with them.
It sounds almost too simple. That's what I thought too — until I tried editing a 40-minute podcast episode in under fifteen minutes.

Descript launched as a transcription tool and quickly evolved into a full-blown editing platform. Today it handles video editing, podcast production, screen recording, clip generation, and even AI voice cloning — all from a single interface. It runs as a desktop app (Mac and Windows) with cloud syncing, so your projects travel with you.
The core promise?
If you can edit text, you can edit video.
No timeline expertise required.
How Descript Works
The workflow is refreshingly linear:
Import or record.
Drop in a video file, an audio file, or record directly inside Descript. You can also record your screen.
Automatic transcription.
Descript's AI transcribes your media with impressive accuracy — usually 95%+ for clear English speech. It identifies speakers automatically.
Edit the transcript.
This is the magic step. Highlight a word, a sentence, or a paragraph. Press delete. The media edits itself to match. Want to rearrange sections? Just cut and paste text blocks.
Filler word removal.
Descript detects every "um," "uh," and "you know" and lets you remove them all with one click — or leave a few in for a natural feel.
Polish and publish.
Add titles, transitions, captions, and effects. Export to 1080p (or higher on paid plans), or publish directly to YouTube, Spotify, and other platforms.

The whole experience feels closer to writing a blog post than traditional video editing. I've watched people who have never touched Premiere Pro produce clean, publish-ready videos on their first Descript session. The learning curve is almost flat.
If you want a detailed walkthrough, check our
step-by-step Descript tutorial
on TonyCiencia.
Standout Features You Should Know About
Descript packs a lot into its interface. Here are the features that genuinely stand out — including some newer additions that push the platform further.
Underlord: Your AI Co-Editor

This is Descript's flagship AI assistant, and it's a big deal. Underlord can handle tedious editing tasks based on your instructions. Tell it to remove filler words, shorten pauses, fix eye contact in your video, or even generate a rough cut from a long recording. It works like having a junior editor sitting next to you — except it's instant.
For creators who batch-produce content, Underlord alone can save hours every week.
Studio Sound

Recorded in a echoey room or a noisy café? Studio Sound uses AI to remove background noise, reduce reverb, and enhance vocal clarity. It genuinely transforms rough recordings into something that sounds like it was captured in a treated studio. I've tested it on audio recorded next to an open window on a busy street, and the result was remarkably clean.
AI Voice Cloning

Here's where Descript gets futuristic. After you record a short voice sample, Descript can generate new speech in your voice. Misspoke a word? Instead of re-recording, just type the correction and Descript's Overdub feature fixes it using your cloned voice. It's seamless enough that listeners won't notice the splice.
This feature alone is a game-changer for podcasters who dread re-recording sections.
Automatic Clip Generation

Descript can analyze your long-form content and suggest short clips optimized for social media. This means one 45-minute podcast episode can become five to ten bite-sized clips for TikTok, Instagram Reels, or YouTube Shorts — with minimal effort on your part.
If you want to explore the latest additions, our guide to
Descript's newest features
covers everything that's been rolling out recently.
Screen Recording + Editing

Descript includes a built-in screen recorder, which means you can capture a tutorial, product walkthrough, or presentation and edit it with the same text-based workflow. No need for a separate screen recording tool.
Collaborative Editing

Teams can work on the same project simultaneously, leave comments on specific words in the transcript, and manage version history. It's built the way Google Docs is built — for real-time collaboration.
Who Is Descript Ideal For?
Descript isn't trying to replace After Effects or DaVinci Resolve for Hollywood-grade color grading. It's built for a specific — and massive — audience:
Podcasters
who want to edit episodes fast without learning a traditional DAW or video editor. If you host a solo show, interview format, or panel podcast, Descript cuts your post-production time dramatically.
YouTube creators
producing talking-head videos, tutorials, or vlogs. The text-based editing workflow is especially powerful for content where speech is the backbone.
Content marketers and social media managers
who need to repurpose long-form content into short clips consistently. One recording becomes multiple assets across platforms.
Course creators and educators
recording lectures, lessons, or training materials. The screen recording plus transcription combo is perfect for educational content.
Small teams and solo creators
who don't have a dedicated video editor on staff. Descript's simplicity means the person who records can also be the person who edits.
Newsletter writers and bloggers
who want to experiment with video or audio without investing months in learning traditional editing software.
If your content involves spoken words — and whose doesn't these days? — Descript deserves a spot in your toolkit. We dive deeper into specific workflows in our
Descript use cases guide
Pricing: Plans That Scale With You
Descript offers a free tier to get started, which is generous enough to let you test the core editing experience with real projects. Here's the breakdown:
| Plan | Monthly | Annual (per month) | Key highlights |
Free
| $0 | $0 | 1 hour of transcription/month, watermark on exports, basic features |
Hobbyist
| $24 | $16 | 10 media hours/month, 400 AI credits, 1080p watermark-free export, Underlord access |
Creator
| $33 | $24 | 30 media hours/month, unlimited AI features, 4K export, AI voice cloning |
Business
| $50 | $40 | Everything in Creator + team features, priority support, advanced collaboration |

The annual billing saves up to 35%, which makes the Hobbyist and Creator plans especially attractive for individual creators. The Hobbyist plan at $16/month is genuinely one of the best values in the creative tools space — you get AI-assisted editing, studio-quality sound processing, and clean exports without a watermark.
If you're ready to try it,
start with Descript's free plan
and see how the workflow feels with your own content.
Why Descript Feels Different From Other Editors
I've used plenty of video editors — from iMovie to Premiere to CapCut. Descript occupies a unique position because it fundamentally rethinks the editing paradigm.
Traditional editors work on a visual timeline. You scrub, you cut, you drag clips. That workflow is powerful, but it has a steep learning curve and demands constant attention to the visual layer.

Descript works on *meaning*. You're reading what was said and deciding what stays. This is how most people naturally think about their content — "I want to cut that part where I rambled" — and Descript lets you act on that thought directly.

The result is faster editing, fewer mistakes, and a much lower barrier to entry. I've seen YouTubers report cutting their editing time by 60-70% after switching to Descript. Podcast producers regularly tell me they went from dreading post-production to actually enjoying it.
For a deeper comparison, our article on
why to choose Descript
lays out the reasoning in detail.
And if you're looking for tips to get the most out of it right away, don't miss our
Descript tips and best practices
The Bottom Line

Descript solves a real problem: video and audio editing has historically been complex, time-consuming, and intimidating for anyone who isn't a professional editor. By letting you edit media through a transcript, Descript makes the process intuitive, fast, and genuinely enjoyable.

The AI features — from Underlord to Studio Sound to voice cloning — aren't gimmicks. They're practical tools that save real time on every project. The pricing is fair, the free tier is usable, and the platform keeps adding features that matter.
If you create any kind of content with spoken words — podcasts, YouTube videos, courses, webinars, social media clips — I'd encourage you to
try Descript for free today
. The first time you delete a paragraph from a transcript and watch the video edit itself, something clicks. It's one of those tools that makes you wonder how you ever edited the old way.

Frequently Asked Questions
Q: Is Descript free to use?

A: Yes. Descript offers a free plan that includes one hour of transcription per month and access to core editing features. Exports on the free plan include a small Descript watermark. Paid plans start at $16/month (billed annually) and remove the watermark while adding more media hours, AI credits, and advanced features like voice cloning.
Q: Can Descript replace Premiere Pro or Final Cut?

A: For speech-driven content — podcasts, talking-head videos, tutorials, interviews — Descript can absolutely serve as your primary editor. It's faster for these workflows than traditional NLEs. For heavy visual effects, complex color grading, or cinematic multi-camera projects, you'd still want a traditional editor. But for 80% of online video content, Descript handles everything you need.
Q: How accurate is Descript's transcription?

A: For clear English speech, Descript typically hits 95%+ accuracy out of the box. It handles multiple speakers well and lets you train it on specific names or jargon. You'll usually need to do a quick proofread pass, but the correction process is as simple as clicking a word and retyping — which still counts as editing the video, thanks to the text-based workflow.

children.text: Is Descript free to use?

Yes. Descript offers a free plan with one hour of transcription per month and core editing features. Exports include a watermark. Paid plans start at $16/month billed annually, unlocking more media hours, AI credits, watermark-free exports, and features like voice cloning.
Can Descript replace traditional video editors like Premiere Pro?

For speech-driven content like podcasts, tutorials, talking-head videos, and interviews, Descript works as a primary editor and is significantly faster. For heavy visual effects or cinematic multi-camera editing, traditional NLEs still have an edge. But for most online video content, Descript handles the full workflow.
How does Descript's AI voice cloning work?

After recording a short voice sample, Descript creates a digital clone of your voice. If you misspoke a word, you can simply type the correct text and Descript generates the replacement audio in your voice using the Overdub feature. The result blends seamlessly with the original recording.
